text.tspan: The Cardinal Spline is a special case of the Hermite Spline.
● The missing tangent vectors are calculated from the neighbour points.
● The positive tension factor s usually has a value between 0 and 1
and affects the tightness of the curve. With the special case of s=0.5
the spline is also called 'Catmull-Rom Spline'.
● A problem arises with the first and last point of an open, non-closed curve.
We don't have two neighbour points here.
● So we emulate the relaxed, tensionless ends of a physical spline
by setting the second derivative of the Hermite spline to zero.
● From here we can continue with the Hermite to Bezier conversion from above.
